name: Vitest
what_it_does: Fast Vite-native unit and integration test framework with a Jest-compatible API, native ESM support, browser mode, and built-in TypeScript execution requiring no separate transform step.
use_cases: Run unit and integration tests in a Vite-based project sharing the same configuration and module resolution as the application
Execute TypeScript tests without a separate build step using native ESM and Vite's transform pipeline
Mock ES modules, timers, and environment globals with a built-in vi mock system compatible with Jest mock patterns
Generate code coverage reports using the built-in c8 or istanbul provider without additional tooling
Run tests in a real browser environment using Vitest's browser mode for testing DOM-dependent code
not_for: End-to-end browser testing across multiple browser engines — use Playwright or Cypress for that purpose
Projects with a non-Vite build system where Jest or another framework is already deeply integrated and migration cost is high
Testing in environments where Vite's ESM-first approach conflicts with CommonJS-only dependencies that cannot be transpiled
best_when: Developing TypeScript or modern JavaScript applications with Vite where fast HMR-like test feedback and zero-config TypeScript support are valued.
avoid_when: The project uses a non-Vite bundler and already has a stable Jest configuration with extensive custom transforms or preprocessors.

auth.notes: Local test runner library with no network auth layer

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Test failure output includes diff views, stack traces with source maps, and clear assertion failure messages; TypeScript errors surface at execution time with helpful context
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Test runs are deterministic and idempotent given the same source code and fixtures; test isolation is configurable per file or suite
known_agent_gotchas: vi.mock() calls are hoisted to the top of the file by Vite's transform; placement in the file does not reflect execution order and can confuse agents generating test files
ESM module caching means vi.resetModules() must be called between tests that modify module-level state
Browser mode requires additional configuration and a browser binary; agents must not assume browser mode is available without explicit setup
Snapshot files (.snap) are generated on first run; agents running tests in read-only environments must pre-generate snapshots
Coverage thresholds cause a non-zero exit code on failure; agents interpreting exit codes must distinguish coverage failures from test failures

security_score_components.security_notes: Local execution only; no network surface area; test environment variables should not contain production secrets; sandbox isolation between test files prevents cross-test contamination
